mnemonics.title: A R S E
mnemonics.content: A R S E - AUTO REGISTER STATIC EXTERN. Think of it as: 'AUTOMATIC cars REGISTER for STATICly EXTERNal races' to remember all four storage classes in order.

mnemonics.title: Static Retains Value
mnemonics.content: STATIC = STAYS AROUND. Static variables STAY in memory throughout program execution, RETAINing their VALUE between function calls.

mnemonics.title: Auto = Automatic Creation/Destruction
mnemonics.content: AUTO = AUTOMATIC. Variables are automatically created when block starts and automatically destroyed when block ends. Think 'AUTO-matic' like automatic doors.

acronyms.expansion: AUTO - Default for local variables, automatic storage duration
acronyms.usage: Used for local variables inside functions that don't need to retain value

acronyms.expansion: REGISTER - Request for CPU register storage
acronyms.usage: Used for frequently accessed variables like loop counters for speed

acronyms.expansion: STATIC - Retains value, static storage duration
acronyms.usage: Used for counters, flags, or variables that must persist across function calls

acronyms.expansion: EXTERN - External linkage, declares variables defined elsewhere
acronyms.usage: Used to share global variables across multiple source files

visualTips.visualization: Picture a program's memory as a cake: CODE at top (smallest), then DATA (initialized globals), BSS (uninitialized globals), HEAP (dynamic), STACK (local/automatic), and REGISTERS (fastest). Storage classes determine which layer.
Think of a light: AUTO is like a motion-sensor light (on only when needed/present). STATIC is like a switch-controlled light (stays on/off once set).
Imagine nested boxes: BLOCK scope is innermost box, FUNCTION scope is the function box, FILE scope is the whole file. Variables can only 'see' inside their box and boxes inside it.

analogies.analogy: Like a whiteboard in a meeting room - created when meeting starts, erased when meeting ends. Only exists for that specific time and purpose.
Like a nameplate on an office door - once placed, it stays there forever (until program ends), retaining whatever name is written.

analogies.analody: Like keeping a notepad on your desk vs in a file cabinet - faster to access but limited space. Compiler decides if it fits.

analogies.analogy: Like a shared document in a team folder - one document (variable) that multiple files (team members) can access and modify.
INTERNAL is like a private note (only you can read it). EXTERNAL is like posting on a team bulletin board (everyone can see and reference it).
